rights.basis: The edition was published in 1979 and no open license or item-specific public-domain determination for its modern editorial contributions was found.

bibliographic.citation: William Dunbar, The Poems of William Dunbar, edited by James Kinsley, Oxford English Texts (Oxford and New York: Clarendon Press, Oxford University Press, 1979), pp. 42-58.
bibliographic.title: The Poems of William Dunbar

entities.description: The immediate printed source named by the U-M XML. Independent critical-edition evidence describes Kinsley's whole poem as based primarily on the early print, with variants from that print and the Maitland Folio. Because the print lacks lines 1-103, another source was required for the opening, but the evidence inspected does not establish whether Kinsley transcribed the manuscript directly or followed an intermediary edition.
entities.label: James Kinsley, The Poems of William Dunbar (1979), pages 42-58

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1979 and no open license or item-specific public-domain determination for its modern editorial contributions was found.

access.accessMethod: The official Pepys Digital Library is a selection and does not list MS 2553.
access.entityLabel: Maitland Folio, Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553, pages 81-96

access.accessMethod: Contact the Pepys Librarian well in advance, identify Pepys MS 2553 and pages 81-96, and provide the requested research details. Appointments are currently offered Monday-Friday, 10:00-13:00 and 14:00-17:00, subject to advance arrangement and the library's limited restoration-period service.
access.entityLabel: Maitland Folio, Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553, pages 81-96
access.format: Onsite consultation of the physical manuscript

access.accessMethod: Email the Pepys Library with shelfmark Pepys MS 2553, requested pages 81-96 or a request for the whole manuscript, intended use, and a request for a quote and publication terms.
access.entityLabel: Maitland Folio, Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553, pages 81-96
access.format: Repository-supplied photography, terms and format by quotation
notes: The current researcher page directs requests for photographic services to the Pepys Librarian; service is limited during restoration, so confirm whether whole-manuscript photography is feasible.
The Pepys Digital Library says its published images must be purchased and publication permission obtained for reuse; request written terms for any newly supplied MS 2553 photographs.

rights.basis: The Pepys Digital Library states that images must be purchased and permission obtained for publication; no quote or license terms for MS 2553 have yet been obtained.
rights.component: New repository-supplied photography and permission to publish it

dateStatements.label: Witness copied
dateStatements.notes: CELM gives c. 1570-85; other descriptions extend Maitland Folio production to 1586.

entities.description: A beginning-to-end manuscript witness of the poem, copied about 1570-1585/1586. Its rubric and attribution occur on page 81 and its explicit, 'Quod maister Williame Dunbar,' on page 96. It contains damaged or corrupt readings and omits three lines preserved by the print, including lines 360-361 in Salisbury's numbering, so it is not textually complete relative to the other witness.
entities.label: Maitland Folio, Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553, pages 81-96

physicalDescription.extent: Poem from opening rubric to explicit on pages 81-96, with internal omissions relative to the early print
notes: This record describes the work-bearing portion, not a complete codicological description of the manuscript.

rights.basis: The manuscript witness was copied in the sixteenth century and the underlying text is medieval.
rights.component: The sixteenth-century manuscript text of the poem

rights.basis: The Pepys Digital Library states that images must be purchased and permission obtained for publication; no quote or license terms for MS 2553 have yet been obtained.
rights.component: New repository-supplied photography and permission to publish it

bibliographic.citation: William Dunbar, [The tua mariit wemen and the wedo. And other poems], [Scotland? : printer of The tua mariit wemen, 1507?], STC 7350; sole surviving exemplar NLS Sa.6, item 10, imperfect.
bibliographic.pages: signatures a3-b3; bound-volume images 177-189 for this poem, with image 189 shared with the following work
bibliographic.title: [The tua mariit wemen and the wedo. And other poems]

dateStatements.label: Printing
dateStatements.notes: Date, place, and printer remain conjectural; typographic difference and NLS collection guidance rule out automatic attribution to Chepman and Myllar.
dateStatements.value: [Scotland? : printer of The tua mariit wemen, 1507?]

entities.description: An undated early printed tract conjecturally produced in Scotland around 1507, preserved as the sole surviving exemplar in NLS Sa.6. The exemplar lacks its first two leaves and transmits poem lines 104-530 on bound-volume images 177-189; image 189 contains lines 527-530 and then begins the following work. It ends with 'quod dunbar.' NLS identifies it as an additional item not printed by Chepman and Myllar.

holding.notes: Item/tract 10 in the bound volume commonly called the Chepman and Myllar Prints.

entities.label: The tua mariit wemen and the wedo, sole surviving early-print exemplar, NLS Sa.6 item 10
notes: The witness is physically incomplete: all before signature a3, corresponding to poem lines 1-103, is absent.

The schema has no early-printed-witness entity type; catalog_description is used for this copy-level printed object, following repository precedent, and the description and holding retain its physical-witness status.

rights.basis: The tract was printed about 1507 and its underlying poem is medieval.
rights.component: The early printed text and historical typography in the surviving exemplar

access.accessMethod: Use the NLS viewer at image 177 for the work or download the complete published 220-image DJVU sequence from Wikimedia Commons.

access.format: HTML page-image viewer and complete DJVU download

localCopies.label: Complete 220-image published digital sequence of NLS Sa.6
notes: Coverage is complete for the published 220-image digital sequence, not for the physical bound object: inspection of images 1 and 220 found printed leaves rather than covers, boards, spine, or flyleaves.
This is a volume-level digital sequence, not a work-only crop.

workPortion.label: The Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo, surviving early-print text
notes: Image 177 begins 'He wil my corse...', poem line 104. Image 189 contains poem lines 527-530 and the explicit, then begins the following work; it is therefore a shared boundary image.

rights.basis: Wikimedia Commons marks the file public domain/Public Domain Mark. NLS's current copyright guidance states that faithful digitizations have the status of the original and waives, under CC0, rights NLS may hold in its digitization layer.
rights.component: The complete 220-image published digital sequence and faithful page images
notes: The legacy NLS microsite carries a 2006 blanket copyright notice, but NLS's updated 2026 guidance warns that older microsite statements may be outdated or incorrect. No modern item-specific NLS rights label was found.

entities.description: A complete downloadable 220-image published sequence of NLS Sa.6, uploaded to Wikimedia Commons from NLS page images. It reproduces printed leaves but not covers, boards, spine, or flyleaves and therefore is not a complete photographic record of the physical binding. The Dunbar poem begins at image 177 with line 104 and ends on image 189 with lines 527-530; the remainder of image 189 begins the next work.
entities.label: Complete 220-image published digital sequence of NLS Sa.6

next_steps: Inspect Kinsley pages 42-58 and the edition's textual introduction or apparatus for an explicit source statement for lines 1-103.
Determine whether Kinsley transcribed Pepys MS 2553 directly or followed an intermediary printed edition before adding any direct manuscript transcription edge.
notes: Rickly establishes that Kinsley presents the whole poem, bases his edition primarily on the print, and records variants from print and Maitland; this does not by itself identify the immediate source of his opening.
openQuestions.question: What immediate source did Kinsley use for poem lines 1-103, which are absent from the surviving early print?

next_steps: Borrow or inspect an authorized copy of Kinsley's 1979 edition and compare pages 42-58 character by character with the XML.
Pay particular attention to italic letters, abbreviation expansions, punctuation, capitalization, apparatus, and paragraph/speech divisions.
notes: Rickly's explicit use of italics for her own expansion letters does not establish Kinsley's convention.
openQuestions.question: What exact typographic and editorial distinctions in Kinsley pages 42-58 were flattened, omitted, or structurally transformed in the U-M XML?

next_steps: Email pepyslibrary@magd.cam.ac.uk with the shelfmark, work pages, request for whole-manuscript coverage if feasible, desired resolution/color, and intended scholarly/reference use.
Request a written quote and separate permission terms for private research, repository reference, and any publication.
openQuestions.question: Can Magdalene College supply a complete high-resolution facsimile of Pepys MS 2553, or at minimum pages 81-96, and what reuse terms and fee would apply?

next_steps: Consult the latest ESTC record and specialist typographical scholarship to test the traditional 'Rouen print' attribution; the currently attached DIMEV, EEBO-TCP, and NLS evidence supports only the cautious '[Scotland?]' formulation.
Preserve the current '[Scotland? : printer of The tua mariit wemen, 1507?]' formulation unless firmer evidence emerges.
openQuestions.question: Where, when, and by whom was STC 7350 actually printed?

next_steps: Ask NLS rights staff to confirm reuse terms for the exact Sa.6 images if a publication requires institution-specific written assurance.
Retain the Commons Public Domain Mark and the current general NLS policy as separate evidence.
openQuestions.question: Will NLS provide a modern item-specific rights statement for the Sa.6 facsimile that reconciles the legacy microsite notice with its current CC0/public-domain guidance?

reviewNotes: The named Chepman and Myllar Prints volume is a bound collection. NLS classifies this tract as an additional item not printed by Chepman and Myllar, so collection context is not treated as printer attribution.

The Maitland witness runs from the poem's opening to its explicit but contains internal omissions relative to the print; the sole surviving early-print exemplar lacks its first two leaves and begins at poem line 104. Source relations therefore carry explicit line-level scopes and do not call either witness textually complete.

The public Internet Archive file whose metadata names Kinsley's 1979 edition is actually, on visual inspection, David Laing's 1834 volume 1. It is retained only as evidence for the Laing collateral edition, never as a Kinsley surrogate.
The XML profile's creation date of 1508 is inherited descriptive metadata, not proof of an exact composition date.
Access and rights statements apply only to the named entity and component; modern editorial layers, manuscript photography, and public-domain source texts are kept separate.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy of University of Michigan CME item DunTwa, a 1993 digital encoding of James Kinsley's 1979 edition, pages 42-58. Rickly describes Kinsley's complete text as based primarily on the sole surviving early print, which lacks lines 1-103, with variants from both the print and the Maitland Folio; the immediate source from which Kinsley supplied the opening has not yet been established. A complete 220-image published digital sequence of the NLS bound volume is publicly downloadable and cached, with this work mapped to images 177-189; image 189 is shared with the following work. No public digital facsimile of Pepys MS 2553 was found.

catalogSummary.title: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo

catalogingSubject.label: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The abstract 530-line Older Scots poem by William Dunbar. This descriptive record is not Kinsley's 1979 edition, the U-M encoding, either textual witness, or the repository XML representation.

contentParts.label: The Tretis of the Tua Mariit Wemen and the Wedo

locators.value: signatures a3-b3; poem lines 104-530; bound-volume images 177-189, with image 189 shared with the following work

notes: The first 103 lines do not survive in the sole early print but do survive in the Maitland Folio manuscript.
No subordinate content parts are created for the six supplied XML range divisions because they are not intellectual divisions of the poem.
contentParts.summary: The complete 530-line poem: an observer's garden frame, speeches by two married women, and the widow's extended speech.

assertion.notes: The poem must predate its conjecturally dated [1507?] early-print attestation, but the print itself is undated and the cataloged year is uncertain.

dateStatements.display: By the conjectural [1507?] first printing; exact date unknown
notes: The XML profile's single year 1508 is inherited approximate metadata and is not treated as an exact composition date.

CELM's older statement that the work was printed at Edinburgh in 1508 by Chepman and Myllar conflicts with current DIMEV/EEBO cataloging and NLS's attribution guidance; it is retained as a competing attributed claim rather than merged into a date range.

assertion.notes: The sole surviving exemplar is imperfect and preserves only lines 104-530, but its existence attests the work.
Place, printer, and precise date are uncertain; the surviving print is not safely attributable to Chepman and Myllar.

extent.description: One complete poem of 530 numbered verse lines. The XML contains 26 LG elements used for speeches and verse paragraphs, seven HEAD elements, and seventeen PB milestones spanning Kinsley pages 42-58. Six DIV1 containers and their line-range heads are explicitly supplied navigation units, not authorial chapters; the poem is continuous rather than regularly stanzaic.

formStatements.description: A continuous poem in 530 unrhymed alliterative long lines, organized as speeches and verse paragraphs rather than a regular stanza sequence.

genres.label: Debate poem
notes: The two married women and the widow exchange contrasting accounts of husbands, desire, and strategies within marriage.

genres.label: Social satire
notes: METS frames the poem as social satire with fabliau-like elements; 'fabliau' is retained as a descriptive comparison, not promoted to a separate controlled genre here.

languageStatements.label: Older Scots
notes: Rickly explicitly describes the language of The Tretis as Middle Scots; this record uses the broader repository display label 'Older Scots' and ISO 639-3 code sco.
The CME XML itself contains no language declaration and is not used as evidence for this statement.
The abstract work is the cataloging subject; James Kinsley is therefore not entered as editor of the work itself, although his edition is the immediate source in the linked lineage manifest.
The Maitland Folio and STC 7350 are two textual witnesses with different survival coverage. They are not merged into a single source entity.
The six supplied XML line ranges and 26 LG elements are encoding/verse-paragraph structures, not an imposed stanza count.
The early print's presence in the volume called the Chepman and Myllar Prints does not justify attribution to those printers.

The current preferred first-printing formulation is [1507?]. The XML year 1508 and CELM's older Edinburgh 1508/Chepman-and-Myllar statement remain attributed competing claims, not endpoints of a synthesized range.

next_steps: Review current Dunbar chronology scholarship and the latest specialist study of the early print's typography and date.
Separate evidence for composition from evidence for first attestation in any revision.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated more narrowly than 'by the conjectural [1507?] first printing' without treating the conjectural print date, inherited XML year 1508, or CELM's older Chepman-and-Myllar attribution as exact?

next_steps: Consult current Dunbar biographies and poem-specific scholarship for a grounded court, city, or itinerary claim.
Retain Scotland only as cultural origin unless evidence distinguishes composition place from author association and print production.
openQuestions.question: Is there reliable evidence for an exact place of composition within Scotland?

assertion.notes: The work is by the Scottish poet William Dunbar and is transmitted in Older Scots; this does not establish a city or court as the exact place of composition.
Pepys MS 2553 gives an attribution at the rubric and an explicit naming 'maister Williame Dunbar'; the early print ends 'quod dunbar.'

subjects.label: Courtly love and marriage
notes: DIMEV indexes a burlesque of courtly love; the poem's sustained subject is the women's experience and manipulation of marriage.

scope.description: The garden drinking gathering frames and enables the women's debate.

summaries.value: On a midsummer night, a concealed male narrator watches two married women and a widow drinking in a garden. Each woman recounts and debates her experience of husbands, sexuality, courtly behavior, and the tactics by which she negotiates marriage; the widow then develops the longest and most worldly account. The overheard female symposium forms an alliterative social satire on marriage and gendered speech.

This record describes one abstract poem. The six DIV1 ranges in the U-M XML are explicitly supplied electronic navigation groups, and its 26 LG elements are variable speech/verse-paragraph groupings, not 26 regular stanzas or six intellectual sections.
